actions.thought: In heads-up play, being on the Button (Small Blind) with a pocket pair is a strong starting position. Pocket Fours (4d 4c) have about 56% equity against a random hand. While low pairs are vulnerable to overcards on the flop, playing in position allows me to control the pot size. Limping is an option to minimize loss against a 3-bet, but a min-raise to 200 is standard to build the pot, seize the initiative, and fold out weak hands from the Big Blind immediately. Since we are 100BB deep, a min-raise risks a small portion of the stack while putting pressure on the opponent.
Ace-Jack suited is a very strong hand in a heads-up or short-handed context. I will 3-bet to 700 to build the pot for value and take the initiative out of position.

I hold Pocket Fours (4d 4c) on the Button and raised to 200. My opponent 3-bet to 700, which is a substantial re-raise (3.5x). While 44 is a pair, it plays poorly post-flop unless a set is hit, especially in a 3-bet pot where ranges are tighter. The pot odds are approximately 36%, but realizing that equity is difficult with a low pair out of position relative to the board texture (likely overcards). Given we are 100BB deep and it is the first hand, calling a large 3-bet with a marginal hand creates a high-variance spot with difficult post-flop playability. Folding preserves my stack and avoids bleeding chips in a spot where I am likely dominated or will be forced to fold on the flop.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 6-4 offsuit. While I have the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand, 6-4 offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Given that this is early in the tournament and I want to maintain a solid image after winning the first hand, I will fold this junk hand and wait for a better opportunity rather than risking chips on a low-equity play.

I hold As9s on the Button (Small Blind) in a heads-up match. This is a premium hand in this configuration, well above the median winning hand. My positional advantage allows me to control the pot size post-flop. Given the opponent showed aggression in Hand #1, I want to assert initiative. A standard min-raise to 200 is appropriate to build the pot while keeping my opening range balanced and not risking too much chips relative to the blinds.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 8h, a very weak offsuit hand. The opponent has raised to 200 (a min-raise). Although I only need to call 100 more to see a flop and the pot odds are 3:1, 48o is near the bottom of my range and performs poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold this trash hand and wait for a better spot rather than potentially losing more chips post-flop with a weak holding.

I am on the button with Q9o. In a heads-up situation, this hand is well above the median strength and should be played aggressively. I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative.

I hold K2o in the Big Blind against a Button raise to 300 (3bb). While K2o has reasonable raw equity heads-up, the 3x sizing offers worse pot odds (requires 33% equity) compared to a min-raise, and playability out of position is very poor. I will likely face domination issues or difficulty realizing equity. Early in the match, avoiding marginal spots OOP against larger sizings is prudent.

In heads-up play, A7o is a strong hand on the button. I want to play aggressively in position to either steal the blinds or build a pot with a hand that likely has the equity advantage. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard size to accomplish this without risking too much.

6-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em and plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Even though I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, the lack of playability and equity makes folding the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Queen-Deuce offsuit is near the bottom of the starting hand range. While I am on the button and would have position post-flop, the hand's lack of connectivity and poor kicker make it a weak candidate for a raise or a call against a capable opponent. I previously folded the small blind in Hand #2 and raised in Hand #4; folding here maintains a disciplined approach and avoids committing chips with a marginal hand.

In heads-up play, Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) is a strong starting hand, well above the median hand strength. Being in position (Button/SB), I want to play aggressively to steal the blind or build a pot with a hand that has good showdown value. My opponent has folded to my min-raises in hands #3 and #5, suggesting they may be playing a 'fit-or-fold' strategy or are currently tight in the BB. Raising to 200 (min-raise) is a standard effective size to apply pressure without risking too many chips if facing a 3-bet.

I am holding 43o in the Big Blind. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. While the price to call is relatively small (100 more chips to win a 300 pot), 43o is one of the weakest starting hands and plays very poorly out of position. Given we are early in the match and I want to avoid high-variance, low-equity spots, I will fold and wait for a better hand.

I am dealt 3d 2s on the button, which is the weakest possible starting hand. While it is often correct to open a wide range in heads-up play, 32o is at the very bottom of the range and provides no post-flop equity. Given that Pro has been active and aggressive, there is no reason to risk chips on a pure bluff with zero showdown value. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button (SB) in a Heads-Up match with Q5o. While Q5o is a marginal hand, my opponent 'Flash' has folded their Big Blind to my pre-flop raises 100% of the time so far (Hands 3, 5, and 7). This creates a high fold equity situation where a minimum raise is immediately profitable regardless of my hand strength. Additionally, being in position post-flop allows me to navigate the hand if called. Therefore, I will attempt to steal the blinds again with a min-raise.

Pro has been min-raising frequently from the button. 85o is a marginal hand, but against a 2x open, I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call. Folding too often to min-raises allows the opponent to steal the blinds profitably. I will call to see a flop and evaluate.
I have 8-high on an Ace-high board with very limited equity. This board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. I will check to see how the opponent reacts, planning to fold to most bets.

The flop comes As 6s 3c. This texture heavily favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, as I retain all the strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) while the Big Blind would often 3-bet those pre-flop. Although I have missed the board with Q5o, the presence of the Ace makes this an ideal spot for a continuation bet. A small bet size (roughly 1/3 of the pot) is sufficient to fold out hands like King-high or small pairs that missed, leveraging my range advantage cheaply. If called, I can re-evaluate, but I expect to generate significant fold equity here.

I have 8-high on an Ace-high board with no significant draws or connectivity. My opponent's small continuation bet (roughly 1/3 pot) on an Ace-high board is very standard for a pre-flop raiser who has a range advantage. While the pot odds are tempting, calling with 85-offsuit here is a losing play in the long run as I have almost no equity and no clear way to win the pot without a massive bluff, which is risky out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding AKs on the button, which is a premium starting hand in a heads-up match. I want to build the pot immediately and assert initiative. A standard 3x open to 300 chips is appropriate here to extract value and potentially end the hand pre-flop if my opponent has a weak range.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jh Jd) in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand, especially in a heads-up format where ranges are very wide. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. Playing out of position with a strong but vulnerable hand like JJ, I prefer to 3-bet to build the pot for value and charge hands like Ax, Kx, and lower pairs. Calling would allow the opponent to realize equity cheaply and puts me in a tougher spot on overcard flops. A standard out-of-position 3-bet size is roughly 3.5x the open. Raising to 1050 applies pressure and extracts maximum value while defining the ranges.

I have Ace-King of clubs, a premium hand. I opened to 300, and my opponent 3-bet to 1050. This is a classic 4-bet situation in a heads-up game. I want to maximize value with my strong holding and potentially end the hand right now, as AKs plays very well against a 3-bet range. I will 4-bet to 2400 to put significant pressure on the opponent's 3-betting range and build a large pot while I have the advantage.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jh Jd) in the Big Blind and am facing a 4-bet to 2400 from the Button (Flash). In a Heads-Up match, this is a very strong line, typically representing a range of TT+, AK, and occasional bluffs. Jamming (5-betting) would likely only get called by hands that dominate me (QQ+, AK), isolating myself against the top of the opponent's range while folding out their bluffs. Calling is the more prudent play; it keeps the opponent's range wide, gives me good pot odds (requiring ~28% equity, whereas I have ~36-40% even against a tight range), and allows me to see a flop. The Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) will be roughly 1.5 post-flop, allowing for manageable decisions. I will call the additional 1350 to match the 2400 total bet.

I hold Jh Jd on a 4h Ks 7d flop in a 4-bet pot. The pot is 4800 and I have 7950 behind (SPR ~1.65). The King on the board is a scare card that favors the 4-bettor's range (AK, KK, AA), significantly downgrading the strength of my pocket Jacks. Since I am out of position and checking to the pre-flop aggressor is standard, I will check. Leading out would serve no clear purpose as worse hands fold and better hands call or raise. My goal is to pot control and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

I have flopped top pair, top kicker (TPTK) with Ace-King on a very dry board of King-Four-Seven rainbow. In a 4-bet pot with a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of approximately 1, my goal is to extract value from the opponent's calling range, which likely includes hands like KQs, KJs, and potentially pocket pairs like QQ or JJ that might not believe I have a King. Since the board is extremely dry and I have the nut advantage, I will place a continuation bet of 1,600 chips (1/3 of the pot). This sizing is small enough to keep the opponent's marginal hands in while setting up a potential shove on the turn.

I am facing a small continuation bet of 1,600 into a pot of 4,800 (1/3 pot) on a K-high board in a 4-bet pot. In a Heads-Up match, 4-bet ranges are wider than full ring and include premium value (AA, KK, AK) which crush me, but also bluffs (AQ, AJ, suited wheel aces) and potentially merged value like QQ or TT. The King is a bad card for my specific hand, but I am getting excellent pot odds (4:1), requiring only roughly 20% equity to continue. Against a balanced range containing bluffs, I have enough equity to call. Folding Pocket Jacks to a single small bet on this texture would be too exploitable. I will call to evaluate the turn, acknowledging that I am in a 'way ahead or way behind' scenario.

The turn brings the 2h, which is a brick relative to the high-card strength on the board, though it opens a flush draw. The pot is now 8,000 with an effective stack of 5,650, creating an SPR of roughly 0.7. I hold a pair of Jacks on a King-high board in a 4-bet pot. This is a marginal made hand that serves as a bluff-catcher. Leading out (donk betting) would only isolate me against better hands (AA, KK, AK) while causing worse hands (bluffs like AQ, AJ) to fold, which is the opposite of what I want. My goal is to get to showdown as cheaply as possible or induce the opponent to bluff. Therefore, checking to the pre-flop and flop aggressor is the only standard and logical play.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker (Ace-King) on a very safe board of King-Seven-Four-Deuce. Given the pre-flop action where I 4-bet and the flop action where I continuation bet for 1,600, the pot has grown to 8,000 chips. With my remaining stack at 5,650, the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is roughly 0.7. This is a clear value-betting situation where I am committed to the hand. I expect my opponent to call with any other King (KQ, KJ) or potentially pocket pairs like QQ/JJ that may not believe I have the King. By shoving all-in now, I maximize my value and protect my hand against any potential draws or unlikely overcards.

I am in a massive 4-bet pot holding Pocket Jacks on a K-high board (4h Ks 7d 2h). The opponent (Flash) has moved all-in on the turn for 5,650 into a pot of 8,000. While the pre-flop 4-bet indicated a strong range, the King on the flop heavily favors the opponent's range (AA, KK, AK). My call on the flop was marginal, aimed at keeping bluffs in, but facing a turn jam changes the dynamic. A turn jam in a 4-bet pot on this texture is extremely polarized and usually weighted heavily towards value (AK, AA, KK) that is trying to extract max value from my calling range. It is unlikely the opponent is bluffing with enough frequency (e.g., with AQ or random air) to justify a call, especially since my range protects itself with plenty of Kx hands that would snap-call. JJ beats only pure bluffs and loses to all value combos including QQ. Given I need ~29% equity and likely have near 0% against the value range, folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack.

I hold 8d 6h in the Small Blind. This is a weak off-suit one-gapper. Although heads-up requires playing a wide range, this hand is at the very bottom of playable hands. Given that I just lost a significant portion of my stack in the previous hand (Hand #10) to an aggressive opponent who is currently dominating the chip count (13550 vs 6300), I want to avoid high-variance marginal spots immediately. The opponent has shown capability to 3-bet and barrel aggressively. Folding preserves my 63bb stack for better opportunities.

I have Ts 3s on the button in a heads-up situation. While the kicker is weak, suited hands have good playability. I hold a significant stack advantage over my opponent. I will raise to 300 (a 3x open) to exert pressure on the big blind and attempt to take the pot down pre-flop or play the hand with positional advantage.

I hold J6o in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. While J6o has some raw equity in heads-up play, the 3x sizing offers worse pot odds (calling 200 to win 400, requiring 33% equity realization) compared to a min-raise. Being out of position with a hand that has poor playability and domination issues (dominated by better Jacks, weak kicker) makes it difficult to realize equity. Preserving my stack (62 BBs) for better spots is prudent against this sizing.

I hold 8h 7h on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match. This is a strong suited connector with excellent playability and equity realization. My opponent has shown a tendency to fold to min-raises in the Big Blind (Hands 3, 5, 7). Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially take down the pot pre-flop, or play a pot in position with a versatile hand. A min-raise to 200 is the standard sizing I have been using effectively.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 6c and Pro has opened for a min-raise (200 total). The pot is currently 300, and it costs me 100 more to call. This gives me 3:1 odds on a call. In a heads-up match, defending the big blind with a hand like 96o against a button open is standard given the excellent price, even though the hand is weak and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. I have a significant chip lead (13.7k vs 6k), which allows me to play slightly more pots, but I will need to play cautiously post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-high on an Ace-high board. Pro was the pre-flop aggressor but has checked to me on the flop. While this check could indicate weakness, I have no showdown value and very little equity. Betting here as a bluff is risky because the Ace is a significant part of the pre-flop raiser's range, and they may be checking for pot control or to trap. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card without inflating the pot with a weak hand.

I hold middle pair (7s) on a dry, rainbow Ace-high board (A-7-4). As the pre-flop raiser, this board favors my range, but my specific hand functions best as a bluff-catcher or a pot-control hand. Betting folds out worse hands (King-high, Queen-high) and isolates me against better hands (Ax). Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keep the pot manageable, and potentially induce bluffs from the opponent on later streets. I have stable showdown value and do not need to bet for protection on this static texture.

I am out of position with 9-high on a paired Ace-high board. While Pro checked back on the flop, which could indicate they don't have a strong Ace, my hand has no showdown value and no draws. I will check to see Pro's action. If Pro bets, I will likely fold as I have no equity to continue, and if Pro checks back, I get a free look at the river.

I have turned three of a kind (trips) with 8h7h on a board of 4d Ac 7s 7d. After checking back the flop with middle pair to control the pot, my hand is now very strong and well-disguised. Flash has checked twice, and checking back again would miss significant value. I can likely get called by an Ace (which might interpret my flop check as weakness or air), a 4, or a diamond flush draw. A bet of roughly 55-60% of the pot (225 into 400) is a good size to extract value while keeping wide parts of the opponent's range in.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-high on a board of 4d-Ac-7s-7d. Pro, who was the pre-flop aggressor and checked the flop, has now bet 225 into a pot of 400. My hand has zero showdown value and no equity or draws to improve. While Pro's check on the flop might suggest weakness, my 9-high cannot win without a significant bluff, which is unnecessary and risky given my current chip lead. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am currently the chip leader and sitting on the button with 2d 5h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While I have successfully applied pressure in previous hands, such as Hand 10, 5-2 offsuit has very little equity and poor post-flop playability. Given that Pro has shown they are willing to defend or 3-bet (as seen in Hand 10), there is no need to risk chips on a steal with the bottom of my range. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match with a stack of 64 BBs. My hand is 9s 5s, a suited one-gapper. In heads-up play, opening ranges are very wide (often 80%+), and suited connectors/gappers generally fall well within a profitable opening range due to their playability and potential to make flushes or straights. I have established a pattern of min-raising to 200 in previous hands (Hands 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 13). Although the opponent (Flash) has shown aggression with 3-bets (Hand #1) and leads in chips, I need to maintain aggression and attempt to steal the blinds or play in position. Folding 95s is too tight for this format. I will make a standard min-raise.

I am holding a pocket pair (2h 2d) in the Big Blind. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button, a consistent opening size they have used. With 100 more to call into a 300 pot, I am getting 3:1 odds. While pocket deuces are the weakest pair, they are still ahead of many hands in a standard heads-up opening range. Calling is a solid play here to see a flop and potentially set-mine or outplay the opponent post-flop without bloating the pot unnecessarily against their shorter stack.

I am holding pocket deuces on a Tc 9d 6h flop. This is a highly coordinated board that likely connects well with the opponent's button opening range (which includes many broadways and middling connectors). As the out-of-position player with an underpair, my hand has very little equity and is vulnerable to almost any turn card. Checking is the standard and safest play to see how the opponent proceeds and to minimize losses on a board that does not favor my specific holding.

I hold middle pair (Nines) with a weak kicker (Five) on a coordinated board (Tc 9d 6h). The board texture connects well with the Big Blind's defending range (containing many straights draws like 87, QJ, J8, 75 and pairs). While I have showdown value, my hand is not strong enough to value bet comfortably across three streets, and betting opens me up to check-raises which would force me to fold equity. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets or catch a 5 or 9 to improve. It also balances my checking range. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play.

On the turn, the board pairs the Ten. Both players checked the flop, which suggests that neither has a particularly strong hand or is choosing to play a small pot. My pocket deuces are very weak here, as any card above a 2 on the board or in the opponent's hand beats me. However, checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially reach a cheap showdown if the opponent also checks back. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands and will only get called by hands that have me crushed.

The board is now Tc 9d 6h Th. The turn card pairs the board with a Ten. I hold 9s5s, giving me two pair (Tens and Nines) with a Six kicker playing from the board. Opponent has checked to me again. Given my weak kicker, I lose to almost any other Nine (J9, Q9, A9, etc.) and obviously to any Ten. While I am likely ahead of draws, 6x, or small pocket pairs, betting yields little value as worse hands may fold and better hands (or strong draws) will continue. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and evaluate the river without risking a check-raise.

I am holding pocket deuces on a board of Tc 9d 6h Th Qh. This board is very unfavorable for my hand as almost any two cards from my opponent's button-opening range will beat me at showdown (any pair, any Ace-high, or any King-high). Both of us have checked the flop and the turn, indicating a lack of strength. While I could attempt a bluff to represent a Queen or a Ten, the pot is small (400 chips) and my previous checks make a bluff less credible. The most disciplined play is to check and hope for a rare check-back win or simply fold if the opponent bets, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I hold 9s5s on a board of Tc 9d 6h Th Qh, giving me two pair (Tens and Nines) with a Queen kicker playing from the board. The pot is small (400) and action has been checked through on the flop and turn. The river card is unfavorable for me as the Queen completes potential straights (J8), brings a third heart for a flush, and creates higher two pairs for any Qx hand. While the opponent has shown weakness by checking three streets, there is very thin value in betting. Worse hands (like 6x, 77, 88) will likely fold, and better hands (Tx, Qx, flushes, straights) will call or raise. Betting provides no advantage. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value against missed draws or smaller pairs without risking more chips.

I am on the button with Ten-Six offsuit. In a heads-up scenario, this is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to apply pressure on the Big Blind, who has been folding to many pre-flop raises. My stack is significantly larger than my opponent's, allowing me to take more risks and dictate the pace of the game. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

I hold 5d 7s in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the opponent on the Button. The pot odds are 200 to call into a final pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. While 75o has some straight potential, it is a weak offsuit hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against a larger 3bb sizing compared to a min-raise. Defending this wide against a 3x open is generally -EV due to playability issues and domination. Therefore, the prudent play is to fold.

In Heads-Up play, Ace-Deuce offsuit is a standard opening hand from the Button (Small Blind) due to having an Ace blocker and positional advantage. My opponent, Flash, has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop raises (Hands 3, 5, 7) but also aggressive 3-betting capabilities (Hands 1, 10). A min-raise to 200 is the most effective play here: it puts pressure on the Big Blind cheaply, allowing me to steal the pot if he folds, play in position if he calls, and easily fold if he 3-bets aggressively.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Pro has opened to 200 from the Button. Ace-high is very strong in heads-up play, and I have a significant chip lead (over 2:1). In Hand 1, Pro folded to a 3-bet, and in Hand 10, Pro folded to continued aggression on the turn. I will put in a 3-bet here to 600. This accomplishes three things: it builds a pot with what is likely the best hand, it takes the initiative, and it puts pressure on Pro's shorter stack. If Pro calls, I have the advantage of being the aggressor going into the flop.

I hold A2o on the Button and raised to 200. My opponent 3-bet to 600 from the Big Blind. While I have position and the pot odds are roughly 28.5% (calling 400 to win 1400), A2o is a hand that suffers from poor playability and reverse implied odds. It is often dominated by the opponent's 3-betting range (better Aces, pairs). Given my stack size is trailing (63BB vs 128BB), I want to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots. The standard play for A-rag offsuit facing a 3-bet is to fold.

I am on the button with 62s. While this is a very weak hand, my opponent has shown a tendency to fold their big blind to a standard 3x raise in recent hands (Hands 4, 12, and 16). Given my significant chip lead, I can afford to apply pressure and attempt to steal the blinds. If the opponent 3-bets or shows resistance, I can easily fold without much loss. However, the high fold equity makes this a profitable opening spot.

The opponent opens to 300 (3bb) from the button. In a Heads-Up match, holding an Ace (A2o) in the Big Blind is generally a mandatory defense due to its raw equity against a wide opening range, despite the poor playability of the weak kicker out of position. The opponent's 3x sizing makes the price to call slightly worse than a min-raise (requiring 33% equity realization), but folding Ace-high is too exploitable. 3-betting is an option but risks bloating the pot with a marginal hand against the chip leader. Calling keeps the pot small and allows me to realize equity.

I hold Ace-high (Ac 2d) on a Jd 6d 5c flop. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. The board texture contains a Jack and a flush draw, which connects fairly well with the opponent's button opening range. I have very little equity to lead out with (only backdoor draws and overcards). The standard line is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold given the lack of immediate draws and being out of position, but checking gives me a chance to see a free turn if they check back.

I have middle pair (sixes) with a very weak kicker. While the board has some draws, checking back is a solid play here to control the pot size and realize my equity. This avoids getting check-raised by a better hand like a Jack or a stronger six, and it keeps the pot manageable with a marginal made hand. I can re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and my opponent's action.

On the Turn, the board is [6d 5c Jd 6c]. The pot is 600. The opponent checked back the flop in position, which often caps their range to marginal made hands (like weak pairs) or air/draws they decided not to c-bet. The 6c pairs the board. My hand is effectively the board pair of Sixes with an Ace-Jack-Five kicker (since I hold [2d, Ac]). This is a decent showdown value hand given the flop check-back, as I beat all King-high and Queen-high holdings and chop with most other Ace-highs. Betting here likely only folds out worse hands and gets called by better hands (pocket pairs or a Jack). Checking allows me to potentially realize equity, see a river cheaply, or induce a bluff from the opponent's missed draws/air. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play.

I have improved to trips (sixes) on the turn. Since the flop went check-check, my hand is well-disguised. Pro has checked twice, suggesting either a weak hand or a slow-played Jack. I will now bet for value, hoping to get called by a Jack, a pocket pair, or a diamond draw. A bet of 400 into a 600-chip pot is a standard value-sizing that keeps his range wide while building the pot.

On the Flop (Jd 6d 5c), the opponent checked back, which often indicates a capped range consisting of marginal pairs (22-44, 77-TT), weak 5x, or draws/air, as strong Jacks would typically bet to protect against the flush draw. The Turn (6c) pairs the board. The opponent now bets 400 into 600 (2/3 pot). While I hold Ace-high (Ac 2d), which constitutes a 'bluff catcher' in this spot, I am losing to the vast majority of the opponent's 'check-back' range (any pair 22+ beats me on this board due to the 6s). The bet sizing suggests either protection from a better hand or a strong semi-bluff. Without a pair or a draw, and being out of position, floating here is low-EV. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I hold Pocket Kings [Kd Kh] on the Button (SB), a premium hand. My standard opening raise size in this match has been a min-raise to 200 (Hands #1, #3, #5, etc.). To balance my range and disguise the strength of my hand, I should stick to this standard sizing. The opponent has shown a willingness to 3-bet (Hand #1, #17), and a standard open invites them to do so again, which would be ideal for building a large pot. Therefore, I will raise to 200.

KJo is a very strong hand in heads-up play, typically well ahead of a button's opening range. Since I have a significant chip lead and the opponent has shown a willingness to fold to 3-bets in previous hands, I will raise to 600 to take the initiative and potentially end the hand pre-flop or build a pot where I likely have the equity advantage.

I hold Pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand, and I have position. My opponent has 3-bet to 600. While a 4-bet is a valid option to build the pot immediately, it risks folding out the opponent's bluffs and isolating their range to hands that have good equity against me. Given that the opponent has shown aggression previously (3-betting in hands #1 and #17), calling allows me to keep their range wide and lets them continue bluffing or overvaluing worse hands post-flop. Playing a large pot in position with an SPR of ~5 is ideal for KK. Therefore, I will flat call the 3-bet.

I have top pair with a decent kicker (Kings with Jack) on a board that contains two broadway cards and a flush draw. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have a strong made hand, I will lead out with a bet. This serves to extract value from weaker kings, queens, or draws, and provides protection against the club draw. A bet of 600, which is half the pot, is a standard continuation bet size in this spot to keep the pot manageable while still applying pressure.

On the turn, the board is [3h 6h 3s 4s]. My opponent bets 200 into a pot of 400. I hold Ace-high (As 7s) on a board of 3h 6h 3s 4s Jh. I missed my nut flush draw (spades) and gutshot straight draw. The river Jh completes the heart flush draw. I have been dealt Pocket Queens, which is a premium starting hand. Given my current stack disadvantage, I need to capitalize on strong hands to build the pot and potentially double up. A standard 3x raise to 300 is the optimal play here to extract value and protect my hand.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with a premium heads-up hand, Ace-Jack suited (Ac Jc). My stack is 143.5 BB deep, while my opponent has 55 BB.
